generations.text:
**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the ghosts of those I’ve lost. The sea roars, and the lighthouse stands as it has for thirty years—sturdy, steadfast, and alone. I have not spoken to you in five, and I do not know if I will live to see the dawn of tomorrow. But if I do, I must say these words, for they are the only ones I have left to offer.

You were a child when you left, Eleanor. A child with fire in her eyes and a heart too proud to ask for forgiveness. I tried, I truly did. I told myself I would wait for you, that I would not let the sea take you from me. But you chose your own path, and I chose mine. You thought I was cruel, that I held you back. But I only held you back because I loved you too much to let the world break you.

I have watched the tides turn, the stars blink, and the seasons change. I have seen the light flicker through the storm, and I have seen the dark swallow the horizon. I have prayed for your return, and I have prayed for your peace. But you did not come. And I did not call.

Tonight, the storm comes with fury. The waves rise like a beast, and the wind sings a dirge. I have no doubt that the sea will claim me, if it must

**[Letterhead: The Lighthouse, Gretna Head, 1887]**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now not with the usual haste, but with the weight of years upon my heart, and the storm that gathers outside as if it were the storm of my soul. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though the sea has not ceased its murmurs, I have not dared to voice my own.

You were young when you left, as I was young when I chose this life. I did not know then that the sea would become both my home and my prison, and that the silence between us would grow like the tide—relentless, indifferent, and ever rising.

I have kept the light burning night after night, through tempests and calm, through the weariness of years and the solitude of the rock. The lamp has never failed, and neither has the duty I took upon myself when I first stood here, a boy with no father, no direction, only the sea before me and the promise of a life built upon its edge.

You were always the light in my life, Eleanor. I remember the way you would run up the stairs to see me at night, your laughter like the wind upon the waves, your eyes full of questions and dreams. You were the child I never had, and the daughter I would never forget. But time, like the sea

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this by the dim glow of the oil lamp, its flame trembling as though it too fears the night to come. The wind howls like a wounded beast, rattling the shutters of this old tower, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very heavens. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, as the storm gathers, I find myself writing as though the silence between us is not the only thing that has been broken.

You were twelve when you left, Eleanor. I remember the way your eyes filled with fire as you stood at the threshold of the door, your hands gripping the edges of your shawl as if it were a lifeline. “You don’t understand,” you had said, your voice shaking with something between fury and sorrow. “You don’t understand what it is to live in this house.” And I could not answer you, for I had no words. I had only the sea, the lamp, and the unspoken truth that I could not bear to see you go.

I have kept this lighthouse for twenty-three years now, and in all that time, I have never once doubted the duty I have sworn to. But I have doubted many things—most of all, myself. I have doubted whether I was fit to be a father, whether I had the right to call myself a man, whether I could ever be the kind of father you deserved. And so,

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a banshee at my window. The sea is restless tonight, and the lighthouse stands as a lone sentinel against the storm. I have not spoken to you in five years, but the storm has made me reckless. I must tell you what I have kept locked away in my heart—what I have buried beneath the weight of silence.

You were ten when you left, Eleanor. I remember the day as if it were yesterday. The sky was the color of bruised fruit, the air thick with the scent of rain. You stood at the door, your eyes wide with something I couldn’t name, and I saw in them the same fire that burned in my own soul. You wanted more than the life I could give you. You wanted the world beyond these cliffs, beyond the sea, beyond me.

I tried to stop you. I begged you to stay. But you were not a child anymore. You were a woman with dreams that stretched farther than the horizon.

And so you went. You took your books, your sketches, your music, and you vanished into the night. I watched you go, and I never looked back. Not once.

But tonight is different. The storm is coming, and I am not sure I will survive it. The waves have been rising all day, and the wind carries a sound that is not natural. It is the sound of something ancient

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years. Five long years have passed since you left this house, and I have not spoken to you since. Time has been a cruel companion, and I fear it has not been kind to us both.

The sea, as ever, is restless. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves have begun to rise, foaming with fury. I have stood at the window, watching the horizon darken, and I fear that the storm which gathers beyond the cliffs may be more than mere weather. It may be the end of me, and I do not know if I shall see the dawn.

I have not written to you, nor have I sent word, for I have no words that can begin to mend what has been broken. I have tried, Eliza, I have tried to be the father you needed, but I fear I have failed. I have been too stubborn, too proud, too afraid to reach out when you needed me most. I have let silence grow between us, and now it is as deep as the ocean.

You were a bright light in my life, a flame that burned too fiercely for the kind of man I am. I could not bear the thought of you growing up in the shadow of my own shortcomings, so I sent you away. I told myself it was for your own good, but I know now that I was selfish. I let

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you with a heart heavy with the weight of years, and with the storm that now roils the skies and threatens the very foundation of this old lighthouse. It has been five long years since our last correspondence, and I fear the silence has grown heavier than the sea on a stormy night. I hope you are well, and I pray that the world has not turned so cruelly upon you as it has upon me.

The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea, which has always been my companion, now roars with a fury that sends shivers through my bones. I have lit the lamp this evening, as I always do, though the light seems dimmer than it should be. The lantern's glow is the only thing that keeps the sailors from their doom, and I would not let it falter, even as the storm threatens to break upon us.

I have not written to you because I feared what I might say. The years have not been kind to me, Eleanor. I have aged, and with age has come a weariness that I can neither explain nor escape. I have watched the tides and the stars, and in their silent wisdom, I have found no answers to the questions that haunt me. I have thought often of you, of your laughter, of your dreams, and of the girl you once were—bright-eyed and full of wonder.

I know that time has changed you, as it

**[Sealed with wax, bearing the crest of the Lighthouse at Blackmoor Point]**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a quill that trembles not from fear, but from the weight of years unspoken. Five years, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years since the last time your voice stirred the air in this keep. You were a child then, full of fire and fury, and I—well, I was a fool, as I have always been.

The wind howls now, like a beast in the night, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very stones of this cliff. I have stood watch for thirty years, and I have seen many storms. But this one… this one feels different. The sky is bruised, the air thick with a silence that chills the bones. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, and I have whispered a prayer to the sea, not for myself, but for you.

I have not written to you, nor have I called, because I feared what might come. You were angry, Eleanor. Angry that I would not let you take the lamp, that I would not let you leave this place. You thought I was holding you back, that I was keeping you from the world. But I was only trying to keep you safe. I thought I was protecting you from the darkness, the loneliness, the choices that lead to ruin. I thought I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since you left, and though the days have worn on, the night still finds me staring into the abyss of the sea, watching the waves crash against the rocks as if they were your footsteps, echoing through the hollows of my soul.

You were once the light in my life, my little star, and I have not forgotten the days when you would run to me with your laughter, your eyes wide with wonder at the world. You were my pride, my joy, my everything. But time, like the tides, has brought change. You grew, as all children do, and with growth came distance. And so, we drifted apart, like ships that once sailed together but were carried by winds that pulled them in opposite directions.

I have not spoken to you in five years, not because I do not care, but because I feared that words would not be enough. That I would only speak of my regrets, of the things left unsaid, and that might break you further. But now, as the storm gathers, I find myself at the edge of the cliff, the wind howling like a banshee, and I realize that I must speak.

Tonight, the sea will rise, and I fear I may not see the dawn. I have spent my life as a lighthouse keeper, guiding ships through the darkest nights, but I have

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in good health and high spirits, though I must confess, I write with a heavy heart. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though I have tried to keep our family ties alive through the occasional letter or token, it seems the distance between us has grown as wide as the sea that surrounds this lonely lighthouse.

Tonight, the wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves rise in fury, as if the very sea seeks to swallow the land. I have been watching the horizon for hours, and the storm is coming—fast and fierce. The light is already flickering, and I fear that I may not be able to keep it burning through the night. I have done my duty as a lighthouse keeper for many years, and though I have always taken pride in my work, I must now face the possibility that this duty may claim me.

I have thought often of you these past years, Eleanor. I have wondered what path you have taken, what lessons you have learned, and what sorrows you may have carried. I have tried to be a father to you, even when our words did not always align. I have not been the man I once aspired to be, and I regret the silence that has fallen between us. Perhaps it is too late for words to mend what has been broken, but I must try.

If I do not return from this night, know that I have

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

My dearest Eleanor,

I write this with the ink of a storm on my hands, the sea howling like a beast that has long since forgotten its name. The wind is not just outside the lighthouse—it is inside me, roaring in the hollows of my bones, in the silence where your voice once lingered. Five years, Eleanor. Five years since the last letter you sent me, and I have not dared to answer. Not because I feared your words, but because I feared my own.

You were ten when you left. A child with a mind like fire and a heart like the sea—wild, untamed, and full of questions. You asked me once, when the storm was at its worst, “Why do you stay here, Papa?” I told you I had no choice. But I lied. I stayed because I was afraid of what would happen if I left. Afraid of what would happen if I let go.

And now, the storm is coming. The sky is a bruise of black, the sea a beast that knows no mercy. I’ve lit the lamp, as I have for thirty years, and it flickers like a dying star. I do not know if I will make it through the night. I do not know if I will make it through the day.

But I thought of you tonight. I thought of the way you used to sit on the steps of

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the wind howling like a banshee at my window, the sea roiling as if it too knows the end is near. The lamps are lit, the beacon burns bright, and I stand here, alone, as the storm gathers strength. I have not spoken your name in five years, not since the day you left me with a letter that felt heavier than the sea. But tonight, as the sky darkens and the waves crash against the cliffs, I find myself compelled to reach out—to you, to the past, to the silence that has grown between us.

You were once my light, Eleanor. I remember the nights you sat by the lamp, your fingers tracing the glass, your eyes wide with wonder as the beacon cast its golden glow across the dark. You were my world, my hope, my heart. And then, one day, you vanished. Left me with a letter that said, *“I can’t stay here. I need to find my own way.”* I believed it. I let you go.

But now, as the storm draws near, I wonder if I made a mistake. I have spent these years watching the sea, listening to its stories, and I have come to understand that the waves do not always retreat. Sometimes, they return with a fury that cannot be denied. And I fear that I have become like the sea—unyielding, unfeeling, a force of

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I trust this letter finds you in good health and high spirits, though I fear it may find you in a state of surprise. For five long years, I have not set pen to paper, nor have I dared to write your name upon the page. The silence between us has been heavy, like the fog that clings to these shores in the dead of winter. Yet, as I sit here by the flickering light of the lighthouse, the wind howling like a vengeful spirit, I feel the weight of my silence more than ever.

You must know, my dear, that I have not forgotten you. Not a single day has passed without my thoughts drifting to the hearth where you once sat, your laughter mingling with the crackle of the fire. I remember the way your eyes would light up at the sight of the sea, how you would race up the steps of the tower to peer through the glass, and how you would call me "Papa" with such affection. Those memories are as vivid as the stars that guide our ships through the darkest nights.

But time, my dear, is a cruel and unrelenting thing. It has worn away the edges of our bond, and I fear that I have been too stubborn to reach out, too proud to admit that I have missed you. I have spent these years in the company of the sea and the wind, my only companions, and I have come to

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a hand that trembles not from fear, but from the weight of years unspoken. Five years have passed since the last time our eyes met, and though I have kept the lighthouse burning through every storm, I have not kept the fire of our bond alive. I pray this letter finds you, for I have no one else to tell you what I must.

The wind howls now, like a banshee, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very earth. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may take me before I can see your face again. I have always been a man of duty, of steadfastness, and I have kept the light for you, even when you did not ask for it. I have kept it for the sailors who would have perished had I not. I have kept it for the ghosts of those who came before me, and for the hope that one day, you might return.

You were young when you left, and I was too proud to beg you to stay. I told myself that you needed to see the world, to find your own path, and that I would wait. But time is a cruel thing, and I have waited too long. I have watched the waves crash against the rocks, and I have watched the dawn rise over the sea, but I have never watched the sunrise with you.

I do not know if you will read this,

**[Sealed with wax, the letter bears the faint scent of brine and old parchment. The ink is dark, the handwriting rough, as though the writer has been gripping the quill with all his strength.]**

---

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

**I write this with a trembling hand, for the storm has come at last, and the wind howls like the wails of a thousand lost souls. The sea, that old adversary, roars with fury, and I fear it may take more than just my life tonight. But I must write, for there are things I have never said, and perhaps, in this final hour, I may yet find the words.**

**Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have passed since I ceased to be the man you once knew. I have not spoken your name in all that time, not because I have forgotten you, but because the memory of you is too sharp, too bitter. I told myself I would wait, that I would not chase you, that I would not beg. But now, as the heavens weep and the waves crash against the cliff like the sound of your footsteps, I find myself unable to hold back.**

**I have always been a man of duty, Eleanor. My duty was to the lighthouse, to the sea, to the ships that passed by in the night. I believed that my purpose was to guide them safely to shore, to stand as a

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The sea has always been a cruel mistress, but tonight—tonight—it seems to whisper to me in a language I do not understand. The wind howls like the wails of the damned, and the waves crash against the cliffs as though they seek to tear down the very foundations of this world. I fear I may not see dawn.

But it is not the storm that haunts me most, my dear. It is the silence between us. Five years, Eleanor. Five years since I last saw your face, since I last heard your voice. The world has turned, and I have remained here, alone with the light, the salt, and the sorrow that has grown like a weed in the cracks of my soul.

I have tried, you know. I have tried to write, to reach out, to tell you of the nights I have spent staring into the abyss, wondering if there was still a place in this world where a father could be forgiven. But the letters never went. They sat, unsealed, on the desk in the keep, yellowing with time, like the old books in the library that I used to read to you when you were small.

You were always my light, Eleanor. Even in the darkest of nights, even when the world seemed to have forgotten us, you were the reason I kept the lamp burning. You were

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper**

*To: My Dearest Clara*  
*From: Your Father*

My dearest Clara,

The ink is still wet on this page, and the wind howls like a wounded beast outside. I’ve been waiting for this moment for five long years, and now that it has come, I find I have no words—only the weight of silence pressing down on me.

The sea is restless tonight, and I fear it may not be the only thing rising. The storm is coming, and I am not certain I will see the dawn. But I must write, for if I do not, I may never speak to you again.

I have not seen you in five years, and I do not know if I have the strength to reach out. But I have thought of you every night, every day, in the solitude of this lighthouse. The waves have always been my company, but now they speak to me of you.

I have no regrets, Clara. I did what I thought was right, and I still do. But I have come to understand that the world is not as simple as I once believed. You were young, and I was not the man I wished to be. I made a choice, and I have lived with the consequences.

I have no more letters, no more promises. But I have this, and I hope it is enough.

With all my heart,  
*Your Father*

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now not with the certainty of a man who has known peace, but with the trembling of one who stands upon the edge of the sea, where the wind howls like a vengeful spirit and the waves rise like the wrath of the gods. I have not spoken your name in five years, and yet it lingers in my mind as if it were carved into the very stones of this lighthouse. I pray that these words, penned in the dim light of a storm-laden night, may reach you, and that they may find their way into your heart.

You were once my light, Eleanor. The light that guided me through the darkest hours of my life, the one who brought me back from the depths of despair. I remember the day you left—how the sea seemed to hold its breath, as if it too mourned the loss of a child. I did not know then, nor do I now, how deeply the sea would take from me, how it would steal not only your presence, but your voice, your laughter, your love.

This night, the sea is restless. The wind has risen to a fury, and the waves have grown in height and rage. The lighthouse, which has stood for generations, is now a mere whisper against the storm. I have no doubt that this night will claim me. I have seen the sea take many lives, and though I have been its guardian, I fear it

My Dearest Eliza,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear the winds of time have carried away the last of our words. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and I have often thought of you, dear child, with a heart full of both sorrow and longing. The sea, ever faithful, has borne the weight of my silence, and I trust that you have found peace in the stillness of your own home.

Tonight, the skies have darkened with a fury that I have not seen in many a year. The wind howls like a banshee, and the waves rise and fall with a vengeance, as though the very sea is weeping. I have lit the lantern, as I always do, and though the light may not reach the shore, it burns with the same steadfast resolve as my love for you.

I have been a lighthouse keeper for many years now, and though the duty is solemn, it has given me purpose. I have watched countless storms pass, and though I have learned to fear the worst, I have also learned to trust in the light. It is a fragile thing, this light, and yet it is all that remains to guide the lost and the weary. I wonder if you still remember the nights we spent by the fire, when the world outside seemed to hold its breath, and the only sound was the crackling of the flames.

I have not spoken of you often, for I fear my words

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the ghosts of the men I once buried in the surf. Five years have passed since you left, and yet the weight of your absence has never lessened. I have kept the lighthouse lit, as I always have, but tonight, the storm is unlike any I have seen. The sea rages, the sky is black as pitch, and I fear I may not see dawn.

I have not spoken to you in so long, and I do not know if you will read this. But I must try. I have spent these years watching the horizon, waiting for a sign, a signal, a moment when the waves might calm and the clouds might part. But perhaps I have been waiting for you all along.

You were once my light, Eleanor. Not the beacon that guides the ships, but the warmth in my heart, the voice that reminded me that I was more than just a man who tends to a lighthouse. You were my daughter, and I have failed you. I should have stood by you, fought for you, not let the sea of my silence drown you. I have no excuse, only regret.

The storm is coming. I have prepared the lantern, the fire, and the provisions, but I fear for my own life. I have no one to call for help, no one to share the burden of this night. I have spent so long alone,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

The sea has been restless these past few nights, and the wind has taken on a voice that sounds like your name. I've spent the last five years in this lighthouse, watching the waves crash against the rocks below, and each time I see the light flicker, I think of you. I've not written to you in five years, and I fear that even now, I may not have the strength to finish this letter. But tonight, the storm is coming, and I feel its breath against the windows, like the ghost of a mother's hand on her child's cheek.

You were once my pride, my hope, my little light in the darkness. I remember the day you left, the way your eyes held a fire that I could not understand, and the way you turned your back on me, as if I were a shadow you could not bear to see. I tried to reach you, to call you back, but you were already gone, and the silence that followed was as deep as the sea.

I have been alone here, with only the wind and the waves for company, and the light that I keep burning by the shore. I have not spoken of you, for fear that my words would be like the storm, bringing pain and sorrow. But now, as the tempest approaches, I feel the weight of years pressing upon my chest, and I know that I may not survive the night.

I have no regrets,

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you now with a heart heavy with years of silence, yet with a resolve that the storm may yet take me before I can say what I must. Five years have passed since our last words, and though I have not spoken of you, I have never ceased to think of you. The sea, ever the companion of my days, has never spoken to me with such a mournful cry as it does now. Tonight, a storm is coming—one that may not spare me.

You were always the light in my life, Eliza, brighter than the beacon I tend each evening. I have kept the lighthouse for many years, and in its glow, I have found solace. But no light has ever shone as brightly as yours. I have missed you more than I can say, and I have longed for the days when our conversations were as endless as the tides.

I have not written to you, nor have I sent word, for I feared what might be said. But now, as the wind howls and the waves grow fierce, I feel the time has come to speak. I have no regrets, no bitterness—only the deep sorrow of a father who has failed to be there for his child. I have not been the man I once hoped to be, and I regret that deeply.

If this storm should claim me, I leave you with this: know

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

My dearest Eleanor,

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a beast outside, and the sea roars as if it knows the weight of what I must say. It’s been five years since you left, and five years since I’ve had the strength to call you my daughter. Tonight, the storm is coming—wild, unrelenting, and I fear I may not see the dawn. And so, I write to you, not with the hope of being heard, but with the hope that if you are still alive, you might hear me.

I have not spoken your name in so long, and yet it lingers in my mind like the echo of a distant bell. I remember the day you left—your small, defiant steps down the path, your eyes filled with fire and sorrow. You were only sixteen, Eleanor, and you had the courage of a thousand storms. I told you then that I would not be the man you needed, and I was right. I was not.

I have spent these years in silence, in the company of the sea and the light. The lighthouse has been my only companion, and the sea, my only judge. I have watched the waves rise and fall, and I have wondered if I would ever see you again. I have not written, nor spoken, nor even dreamed of you, for fear that if I did, I might

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy as the storm clouds that now gather over the cliffs. The sea howls like a beast in pain, and the wind screams as if it too remembers the days when I once knew how to love. Five years, Eliza. Five long, bitter years since you left me—left us—without a word, without a glance. I’ve been a lighthouse keeper these years, but I fear I’ve long since lost my way.

You were never meant to leave. Not like this. Not with a silent goodbye and a closed door. I remember the night you did it. The moon was full, and the sea was calm, but there was a storm in your eyes. You said nothing, only turned and walked away, leaving me standing there like a fool, clutching the lantern as if it could light the way back to you.

I’ve kept this lighthouse, but I’ve also kept my silence. I’ve watched the waves crash against the rocks, I’ve heard the cries of the gulls, and I’ve counted the hours in the dark, wondering if I’d ever see your face again. You were my light, Eliza. My only light. And now, as the tempest approaches, I wonder if I’ve been keeping this beacon for you—or for myself.

The storm is coming, and I fear it may take more than the sea. I’ve been alone

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you in good health and high spirits, though I fear it may find you in a state of confusion, for I have not written to you in five long, cruel years. Time, as it has a way of doing, has worn away the edges of our once-pleasant correspondence, and I find myself now, in the solitude of this lighthouse, with nothing but the howling wind and the distant crash of waves to keep me company.

You may wonder why I write to you now, when the last time we spoke was with such bitter words and unkind intentions. I can only say that the weight of silence has grown heavier upon my heart, and the storm that now looms on the horizon has stirred something in me—a longing to speak, to confess, to beg your forgiveness, if it is still within your power to grant it.

You see, Eliza, I have been a lighthouse keeper for many years now, and this tower has been my home, my refuge, and my prison. I have watched the tides rise and fall, the moon wax and wane, and the seasons change, yet none of it has brought me the peace I once thought I would find in the solitude of the sea. I have been a solitary man, yes, but not alone in my sorrow. I have carried the burden of my past, of the words I left unsaid, and the love I once thought I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The sea has been restless these past few nights, and the wind howls like a beast in pain. I have watched the waves rise and fall, and I have seen the sky darken as if the heavens themselves weep. Tonight, the storm is coming—great, terrible, and unrelenting. I do not know if I shall see dawn.

You were never meant to be my daughter. I took you in when you were but a child, with a broken heart and a promise to the sea. You were a storm in yourself, Eleanor—wild, unyielding, and full of questions I could not answer. I tried to raise you with discipline, with purpose, with the same quiet strength that has kept this lighthouse standing for thirty years. But you were never content with the life I gave you. You wanted more than the sea, more than the light, more than the silence of the rocks.

You left without a word, and I let you go. I told myself it was for your own good. That you would find your own path, your own place in the world. But the years have passed, and I have grown old, and the sea has grown crueler. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I do not know if I will ever again.

But tonight… tonight I am not so sure I will survive. The wind

**[Envelope Addressed to: My Dearest Eliza, 123 Beacon Hill, Portsmouth, England]**

**My Dearest Eliza,**

It is with a heart heavy as the storm clouds that gather over the sea this evening that I take up my pen. Five years have passed since the last time our hands met, and yet the memory of your laughter still lingers in my mind like the scent of the sea after a storm. I write to you now not only because the winds grow fierce and the sea roars with a fury that has not been seen in many a year, but because I fear I may not have the strength to see another dawn.
